Q1: I tried to keep things organized, but honestly with limited resources and working part-time, my study routine was pretty scattered. I'd usually set aside a few hours on weekends and maybe two evenings during the week, totaling around 8 hours. I'm a read-write learner, so I'd focus on taking notes and reading through materials when I could. It wasn't perfect, but I made it work with what I had.
Q2: My pattern definitely varied a lot depending on what was due and what else was going on in my life. Some weeks I'd be consistent, but then a shift at work would change or something would come up, and suddenly I'm cramming before a deadline. I tried to stick to a routine, but life doesn't always cooperate when you're juggling multiple responsibilities with limited support.
Q3: Usually it was either a deadline approaching or getting a notification about new content that got me to log in. Sometimes I'd check in just to see if there were any announcements or if I'd missed something important. The gamified tasks actually made me curious sometimes—seeing those badges or points would prompt me to check what was new.
Q4: There was a period mid-semester where logging in became more automatic for me. I'd check the LMS in the morning before heading out, kind of like checking email. The gamified elements helped with that—I liked seeing my progress tracked visually. But that routine broke down when things got hectic toward the end of the semester.
Q5: The reading materials and written resources were most useful for me since that's how I learn best. I appreciated when instructors posted detailed notes or study guides I could download and annotate. The discussion forums were also helpful—I posted there quite a bit and learned from seeing how other students approached problems. Those 14 posts I made weren't just for participation points; I genuinely found value in the exchanges.
Q6: I'll admit I didn't watch as many videos as I probably should have. I'd start them but often wouldn't finish because I learn better from reading anyway. Some of the optional supplementary materials I skipped entirely—not because they weren't valuable, but because I had to prioritize what was absolutely necessary given my time constraints.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time when I could see a direct connection to what would be on exams or assignments. The gamified tasks were interesting because they made even routine work feel like I was accomplishing something. If something helped me understand a concept I was struggling with, that was time well spent regardless of whether it was required.
Q8: Optional activities felt like a luxury I couldn't always afford. Required ones got done because they had to, but optional stuff? I'd look at them and think 'maybe later' and later never came. The gamified optional tasks were different though—they felt more like a game than extra work, so I engaged with those more than I would have otherwise.
Q9: Absolutely. I downloaded almost everything I could so I could study offline. My internet at home isn't always reliable, and I can't afford to hang out at cafes with WiFi all day. I'd print out notes when I could access a printer at the library and study from those. A lot of my actual learning happened with physical papers spread out on my bed.
Q10: There were many times I'd be reviewing my handwritten notes or re-reading printed materials for hours, but none of that showed up anywhere in the system. I'd work through problems on paper, discuss concepts with a classmate over the phone, or quiz myself using flashcards I made. The LMS only saw me when I was online, which wasn't the whole picture.
Q11: All the time. I'd download a PDF or save a webpage, and then that would be my study material for days without logging back in. I watched some videos once, took detailed notes, and never needed to rewatch them because I had everything written down. It was more efficient for me to work offline given my situation.
Q12: Around midterms, things got really hard. I was surprised by how quickly the workload piled up, and I felt like I was constantly playing catch-up. My job scheduled me for more hours, and I couldn't say no because I needed the money. That's when my assignment completion started slipping, and I couldn't give the course the attention it deserved.
Q13: Definitely. When I was exhausted from work or stressed about money, the last thing I wanted to do was log into the LMS and face all the things I was behind on. Fatigue made everything harder—I'd read the same paragraph five times without absorbing it. Competing responsibilities meant the LMS often got pushed to the back burner.
Q14: Yeah, there were times I'd be scrolling through materials but my mind was somewhere else entirely. I'd have the LMS open but be thinking about bills or work schedules. I'd click through pages without really processing anything. Those sessions probably showed up as engagement, but I wasn't really there mentally.
Q15: I postponed the harder assignments more than I should have. I knew they were important, but when you're tired and overwhelmed, it's easier to convince yourself you'll do it tomorrow. Some readings I avoided because they were dense and I knew they'd take energy I didn't have. I regret that now, seeing my final grade.
Q16: Deadlines were basically my external motivation system. I'd see a deadline approaching and suddenly find the energy to get things done. My LMS activity probably spiked right before due dates. Without deadlines, I might have drifted even more. They kept me somewhat on track even when everything else was chaotic.
Q17: When I got feedback, especially positive feedback, it motivated me to keep going. But sometimes feedback came too late to really help me improve on the next assignment. The gamified feedback—like earning badges—gave me little boosts of encouragement. I wished there was more immediate feedback on whether I was understanding things correctly.
Q18: The course structure was pretty straightforward, which helped. Having clear modules and knowing what was expected each week gave me a framework to work within. But eight online courses is a lot to manage, and sometimes the structures of different courses conflicted with each other. I had to constantly prioritize.
Q19: The gamified elements made engagement easier—they added a layer of interest to otherwise routine tasks. But some aspects made it harder, like when resources weren't downloadable or when discussions required real-time participation. For someone with my schedule and limited resources, flexibility in how I could engage would have helped a lot.
Q20: I think that could happen when someone is trying to figure things out but struggling to actually complete the work. Maybe they're exploring, looking for help, or just feeling lost. High activity doesn't mean high understanding. Someone might be clicking around desperately trying to make sense of things but not able to pull together a finished assignment.
Q21: That resonates with me somewhat. I did a lot of studying offline that wouldn't show up in my activity logs. If someone has good study habits outside the LMS or uses other resources effectively, their low online activity might not reflect their actual effort. Though in my case, my exam scores show I probably needed more help than I was getting.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns—when you're active, what you click on, how often you participate in discussions. But they miss the quality of that engagement and everything that happens offline. They can't measure understanding, effort, or the obstacles someone is facing. A log sees actions, not intentions or circumstances.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about learning the material and putting in genuine effort to understand it. It's not just going through the motions or clicking boxes. For me, engagement is when I'm actively thinking, questioning, and trying to connect new information to what I already know. It's mental investment, not just time spent.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how hard I was trying despite my circumstances, or how much I was learning through offline methods. It wouldn't capture the conversations with classmates, the late nights with printed notes, or the genuine interest I had in the subject. It also wouldn't show that I was surprised and disappointed by my performance—I thought I was doing better than my grades reflected.
